title: Early Americans Pioneered the Keto Diet
gist: A Science Advances study argues the earliest Americans were near-carnivores: across three continental regions at least 98 percent of Paleoindian food came from megafauna like mammoths and ground sloths, a meat-and-fat 'keto' diet that enabled rapid expansion until large-mammal extinctions forced diversification.

insights.claim: By about 16,000 years ago humans had spread rapidly through the Americas, from the Clovis people centered in New Mexico to the Fishtail Projectile Point people in Patagonia.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Timeline and geography of the human peopling of the Americas.

insights.claim: A Science Advances study argues megafauna such as woolly mammoths and giant ground sloths sustained Paleoindians, and that a focus on megaherbivores facilitated rapid human expansion into new ecosystems.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Resolves a long-standing debate toward big-game hunting over broad foraging.
insights.quote: focus on megaherbivores facilitated rapid human expansion into different ecosystems
insights.locator: A recent study published in Science Advances

insights.claim: Across the Eastern Beringia, Clovis, and Fishtail Projectile Point regions, at least 98 percent of the food came from large mammals.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A striking quantitative claim about dietary reliance on megafauna.
insights.locator: They found that for all three regions

insights.claim: Butchery evidence shows Paleoindians ate meat and fat from freshly killed animals but did not process bones for marrow, a strategy implying megafauna were plentiful.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Behavior signals abundance of large prey, not scarcity.
insights.locator: Based on the evidence of butchery

insights.claim: Diets shifted with geography: Beringia entrants (13,300-14,000 years ago) ate woolly mammoth, Clovis people (12,800-13,400 years ago) shifted to Columbian mammoth plus mastodons, and Fishtail populations tracked ground sloths, gomphotheres, camelids, and equids.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Maps a regional dietary sequence across the peopling of the Americas.
insights.locator: The study traces the dietary pathway

insights.claim: Early Paleoindians ate a mostly meat diet of protein and fat, with only opportunistic consumption of easily obtained fruits or nuts.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Characterizes the diet as near-carnivorous, a natural 'keto' regime.
insights.quote: these highly mobile Early Paleoindians apparently consumed a diet mostly of meats, including both protein and fat

insights.claim: Large-mammal extinctions later forced Paleoindians to diversify their diets away from megafauna.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Extinction as the driver of dietary broadening.
